figures.gloss: The goddess of the night sky, representing the infinite expanse of the universe and the ultimate matrix of all possibility. She is the continuous, ecstatic joy of existence, inviting humanity to fulfill its desires rather than suffering ascetic restriction.
figures.facet: She aggressively subverts the 'Sky Father / Earth Mother' binary by being a feminine sky, and replaces the demand for purity and renunciation with a demand for ecstatic fulfillment.
figures.provenance: Liber AL vel Legis, Chapter 1, directly adapted from the Egyptian Nut and heavily influenced by 19th-century Swinburnian aestheticism.

figures.gloss: The infinitesimally small, omnipresent point of consciousness; the active, animating spark within every human being. If Nuit is the circumference of all things, Hadit is the center—the 'flame that burns in every heart of man.'
figures.facet: He completely internalizes divinity. He is not a god out there to be worshipped, but the secret, mobile, indestructible viewpoint of the individual practitioner themselves.
figures.provenance: Liber AL vel Legis, Chapter 2, conceptually tied to the winged sun-disk of Behdet (Horus) and Hindu concepts of the Atman.

figures.gloss: The Crowned and Conquering Child, the martial and solar deity presiding over the present 'Aeon of Horus'. He represents strength, active force, and the unapologetic assertion of the individual's True Will in the material world.
figures.facet: He explicitly abolishes the 'Aeon of Osiris'—the era of the dying-and-rising god, self-sacrifice, and guilt—replacing it with an era of aristocratic self-reliance and joyous, unrepentant power.
figures.provenance: Liber AL vel Legis, Chapter 3, syncretized from the Egyptian Ra-Harakhty and heavily infused with Nietzschean philosophy.

figures.gloss: The Scarlet Woman and the Mother of Abominations. She is the sacred harlot who rides the Beast, accepting all experience—joy and sorrow alike—into her Grail, requiring the initiate to spill every drop of their ego's blood into it.
figures.facet: She reclaims the apocalyptic villainess of the Book of Revelation, transforming her 'harlotry' from sin into the ultimate spiritual virtue: the absolute, unrestricted surrender of the self to the universe.
figures.provenance: The Book of Revelation (inverted), John Dee's Enochian system, and Crowley's 'The Vision and the Voice' (Liber 418).

figures.gloss: The discarnate, preterhuman intelligence that dictated 'The Book of the Law' to Aleister Crowley in Cairo in 1904. He acts as the dark, silent minister of Hoor-Paar-Kraat and the primary transmitter of the Thelemic current.
figures.facet: He dissolves the boundary between objective external revelation and psychological integration; Crowley eventually identified Aiwass not just as a distinct entity, but as his own Holy Guardian Angel.
figures.provenance: The Cairo Working of 1904, heavily shaped by the Golden Dawn's concept of the Secret Chiefs and the Holy Guardian Angel.

figures.gloss: The fundamental, uncontaminated trajectory of the individual self. It is the absolute, mathematically precise course a person must follow, free from the distortions of social expectation, moral conditioning, or conscious ego.
figures.facet: It completely relativizes morality. There is no universal standard of 'good' or 'evil'—sin is defined solely as restriction or deviation from one's own unique, inherent orbit.
figures.provenance: Crowley's central philosophical distillation, echoing Francois Rabelais' Abbey of Theleme ('Fay ce que vouldras') and Eliphas Levi.

figures.gloss: The Dweller in the Abyss, a hostile force representing dispersion, confusion, and the last desperate clinging of the human ego. He is the guardian of the threshold who must be overcome to attain the grade of Magister Templi.
figures.facet: He has no true ontological substance; he is not an anti-god or a fallen angel possessing power, but the personification of illusion and the fragmented, self-contradictory nature of the lower mind.
figures.provenance: John Dee's 16th-century Enochian diaries, dramatically expanded upon by Crowley during his North African desert workings in 1909.

figures.gloss: A solitary, deeply psychological ritual of eucharistic auto-initiation. The practitioner cuts their own chest to produce blood, baking it into a 'Cake of Light' which is then consumed to fortify the True Will.
figures.facet: It replaces the communal, congregational worship of an external savior with a private, transgressive self-sacrifice, where the magician acts as both the priest and the sacrificed god.
figures.provenance: Crowley's Liber 44 (1913), adapting Catholic eucharistic forms into a strictly left-hand path, solitary working.

figures.gloss: The Prophet of the New Aeon (1875-1947), To Mega Therion, who received the Book of the Law in Cairo in April 1904 and built out its implications into the A∴A∴ curriculum and a reformed Ordo Templi Orientis over the following four decades.
figures.facet: Named in seven of this roster's eight provenance lines yet absent as an entity: Thelema is, in the plainest sense, his system — every deity, force, and rite here passes through his hand before it reaches the page.
figures.provenance: Aleister Crowley, 'The Equinox of the Gods' (1936) and 'The Confessions of Aleister Crowley' (1929); Richard Kaczynski, 'Perdurabo: The Life of Aleister Crowley' (2010).

figures.gloss: The 220-verse text dictated to Crowley by Aiwass over three days in Cairo, April 1904; its three chapters speak in the voices of Nuit, Hadit, and Ra-Hoor-Khuit respectively, and its central injunction — 'Do what thou wilt shall be the whole of the Law' — is the governing law of the Aeon of Horus.
figures.facet: It is cited by every deity entry in this roster as their source, yet had no entry itself: the scripture does not describe Thelema's gods from outside, it IS their direct first-person speech, chapter by chapter.
figures.provenance: Aleister Crowley (recipient), 'Liber AL vel Legis, The Book of the Law' (dictated Cairo, April 1904; first published with Crowley's Comment 1938).

figures.gloss: Crowley's eucharistic ceremony, written in 1913, enacted by a Priest, a Priestess, and a congregation as the central public sacrament of the Ecclesia Gnostica Catholica; it stages the Nuit-Hadit cosmology bodily, week after week, in OTO bodies worldwide.
figures.facet: Where the Mass of the Phoenix is the solitary magician's private auto-sacrifice, the Gnostic Mass is Thelema's one genuinely congregational rite — the tradition's only sacrament built for a community rather than a single practitioner alone in a temple.
figures.provenance: Aleister Crowley, 'Liber XV, Ecclesiae Gnosticae Catholicae Canon Missae' (1913); ongoing Ecclesia Gnostica Catholica / OTO liturgical practice.

figures.gloss: The personal divine intelligence whose Knowledge and Conversation — attained through the Abramelin operation or Crowley's own Liber Samekh — constitutes the first summit of the Great Work in Thelemic practice, described by Crowley as 'the essential work of every man'.
figures.facet: It collapses the distinction between an external revealer and an inward higher self: Crowley himself came to identify Aiwass, the entity who dictated Liber AL, as his own Holy Guardian Angel, so the tradition's founding revelation and its central initiatory doctrine are the same relationship seen from two sides.
figures.provenance: Abraham von Worms (ed./trans. S.L. MacGregor Mathers), 'The Book of the Sacred Magic of Abramelin the Mage' (1898); Aleister Crowley, 'Liber Samekh' (1921, rev. 1926); doctrine inherited directly from Crowley's training in the Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn.

figures.gloss: The Beast of Revelation reclaimed as the solar-phallic office of the Aeon's prophet and the consort of Babalon, whose Scarlet Woman rides him; Crowley adopted the title as his own magical name and grade-name as Magus.
figures.facet: Babalon's presence in this roster without her consort left the pairing doctrinally half-told — the two offices are inseparable in Thelemic cosmology, the Beast supplying the active force the Scarlet Woman receives into her Cup.
figures.provenance: Liber AL vel Legis III:34 ('let it be his child & that stranger, and let him not know her'); Aleister Crowley, 'Magick in Theory and Practice' (1929); Crowley's lifelong self-identification with the title from adolescence onward.

figures.gloss: The Lord of Silence — babe on the lotus, finger at his lip — the concealed, passive aspect of the double-god Heru-ra-ha, whose active face is Ra-Hoor-Khuit; named in this roster's own Aiwass entry as Aiwass's master ('minister of Hoor-Paar-Kraat') but never itself given an entry.
figures.facet: Silence here is not absence but the interior of force: the same double-god that conquers openly as Ra-Hoor-Khuit also withholds and conceals as Hoor-paar-kraat, a single deity split into an active public face and a silent private one.
figures.provenance: Aleister Crowley, Liber AL vel Legis and 'Liber Israfel' (Liber DCCLXIX), identifying Heru-ra-ha's dual aspect; iconography inherited from the Hellenistic-Egyptian god Harpocrates.

figures.gloss: The 26th-dynasty funerary stele of the priest Ankh-ef-en-Khonsu (Cairo Museum, CG 9422), showing Nut arched over the winged disk and Ra-Horakhty enthroned; its rediscovery by Rose Crowley in a Cairo museum in 1904 was the physical trigger of the Cairo Working.
figures.facet: A relic that becomes doctrine: the stele is not merely the occasion of the revelation but is itself named and addressed inside Liber AL as 'that stele they shall call the Abomination of Desolation' — an artifact written into its own scripture as a canonical object.
figures.provenance: Cairo Museum catalogue item CG 9422 (26th Dynasty); Aleister Crowley, 'The Equinox of the Gods' (1936), recounting its rediscovery in March 1904.

figures.gloss: Crowley's first wife, whose trance utterances in Cairo in March 1904 ('they are waiting for you') directed him to the god Horus and to the Stele of Revealing, precipitating the dictation sessions with Aiwass that produced Liber AL.
figures.facet: Half the tradition's origin story is hers rather than Crowley's or Aiwass's: it is Rose's mediumship, not Crowley's own magical initiative, that identifies the god and the relic and sets the entire revelation in motion.
figures.provenance: Aleister Crowley, 'The Equinox of the Gods' (1936); Richard Kaczynski, 'Perdurabo: The Life of Aleister Crowley' (2010); Lawrence Sutin, 'Do What Thou Wilt: A Life of Aleister Crowley' (2000). Honored in Thelemic tradition as the first Scarlet Woman.

edges.note: The infinite expanse and the infinitesimal point; the ultimate cosmological dyad of Thelema.
Babalon represents the successful surrender of ego into the Cup, while Choronzon is the failure, the ego resisting and dispersing in the Abyss.
GOLD SEAM: Crowley explicitly synthesized his martial lord of the new aeon directly from the Egyptian Ra-Harakhty.
GOLD SEAM: Crowley explicitly correlated the True Will with the Egyptian concept of Ma'at, where the soul is weighed against its own absolute truth.
GOLD SEAM: Aiwass functionally assumes the role of the ultimate esoteric scribe and divine messenger, explicitly identified by Crowley with Thoth.
GOLD SEAM: The Mass of the Phoenix is a conscious, systematic inversion of the Christian Eucharist, redirecting the sacrifice from Jesus to the practitioner's own ego.
GOLD SEAM: Nuit mirrors Prakriti as the vast, passive matrix of all possibility that is only animated by the active point of consciousness (Hadit/Purusha).
GOLD SEAM: Nuit (the encompassing circle) and Hadit (the core point) map directly onto the foundational passive and active cosmic binaries.
GOLD SEAM: Both conceptualize the divine not as an external ruler, but as a hidden, indestructible core of light housed deep within the individual.
GOLD SEAM: The sacred, internal flame that acts as the active, animating principle of life and cosmic order.
GOLD SEAM: Both represent an effortless, frictionless alignment with one's fundamental nature and the overarching cosmic trajectory.
GOLD SEAM: Both are personifications of egoic illusion and dispersion that attempt to prevent the seeker from crossing over to liberation.
GOLD SEAM: The ultimate spiritual adversary, though in Thelema it is a force of psychological dispersion rather than an independent evil creator.
GOLD SEAM: Both are fiercely independent, sexually liberated divine females who master the domains of both heaven and the underworld.
GOLD SEAM: The fallen or exiled divine feminine whose descent into matter and 'harlotry' is the very mechanism of spiritual redemption.
GOLD SEAM: Discarnate, preterhuman intelligences that transmit revolutionary, foundational texts of esoteric law to a chosen earthly scribe.
GOLD SEAM: Both are fiercely martial, solar deities demanding energetic participation and representing an unapologetic ethos of strength.
GOLD SEAM: Thelema's 'Aeon of Horus' explicitly relies on the turning of vast, epochal cycles of human consciousness, succeeding previous paradigms.
Intra-tradition: Crowley is the scripture's recipient and, by the tradition's own account, its Prophet.
Intra-tradition: To Mega Therion is Crowley's own assumed magical title and grade-name, adopted from adolescence and formalized in Liber AL.
Intra-tradition: first wife and catalyst of the Cairo Working that produced Liber AL.
Intra-tradition: the stele's rediscovery triggered the dictation, and the text itself names the stele within its own verses.
Intra-tradition: Rose's trance identification of Horus led directly to locating the stele in the Cairo museum.
Intra-tradition: consort pair doctrinally inseparable in Thelemic cosmology — the Beast the Scarlet Woman rides.
Intra-tradition: the silent and active faces of the single double-god Heru-ra-ha.
Intra-tradition: Crowley came to identify Aiwass, dictator of Liber AL, as his own Holy Guardian Angel — founding revelation and central initiatory doctrine folded into one relationship.
Intra-tradition: the Mass's Priest-Priestess sacrament stages the Nuit-Hadit cosmology as public liturgy.
Intra-tradition: the Priest's role in the Mass enacts Hadit, the animating point, within Nuit's encompassing circle.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Crowley's silent Lord explicitly draws on the Hellenistic-Egyptian Harpocrates (Horus the Child) already venerated in the Isis mysteries, matching this roster's existing Ra-Hoor-Khuit/Ra and Aiwass/Thoth syncretisms.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: direct doctrinal inheritance — Crowley imported the Golden Dawn's Holy Guardian Angel doctrine wholesale into the A∴A∴ curriculum as the Great Work's first summit, per this roster's own Aiwass provenance.

Both a personal, indwelling higher intelligence to be contacted and aligned with as the aim of inner work — the Golden Dawn/Thelemic HGA and the New Age Higher Self as parallel modern reworkings of the guardian-spirit idea.

Both a single prophet-founder whose visionary reception becomes the entire doctrinal and institutional charter of the tradition that follows him — structural parallel of the solitary-revelator pattern, no lineage claimed.

Both modern occult founder-prophets who built a reformed initiatory order (OTO/A∴A∴; Temple of Set) around a discarnate revelation received directly by themselves — parallel careers in 20th-century ceremonial magic, not shared lineage (Aquino's Set explicitly rejects the Satan/Thelema framing).

Crowley explicitly identified Aiwass as his own Holy Guardian Angel, collapsing the Golden Dawn/Abramelin attainment-goal into the entity that dictated Liber AL — stated in his own writings, not an outsider's analogy.

dossier.essence: The religious-magical philosophy proclaimed by the English occultist Aleister Crowley (1875-1947) after he received 'The Book of the Law' (Liber AL vel Legis) in Cairo in April 1904, allegedly dictated by a discarnate intelligence called Aiwass. Its law is 'Do what thou wilt shall be the whole of the Law. Love is the law, love under will,' and its central claim is that each individual has a unique 'True Will' whose discovery and enactment is the sole duty of life. It is carried by the initiatory orders A.'.A.'. and Ordo Templi Orientis (OTO), which Crowley reorganized around it, and remains active worldwide.
dossier.cosmos: A new 'Aeon of Horus' has succeeded the Aeons of Isis and Osiris, ending the era of self-sacrifice and inaugurating an age of self-realization. The cosmology of Liber AL turns on three deities: Nuit, the infinite star-goddess of all possibility; Hadit, the infinitesimal animating point 'in every heart'; and their child Ra-Hoor-Khuit, martial lord of the Aeon. The Great Work is the union of the magician with the 'Holy Guardian Angel' and the crossing of the Abyss — guarded by the demon of dispersion, Choronzon — to attain the divine.
dossier.practice: Thelemic practice is 'Magick' — 'the Science and Art of causing Change to occur in conformity with Will' — combined with yoga and rigorous record-keeping; disciplines include the solar adorations of Liber Resh and the solitary Mass of the Phoenix, while OTO's central public rite is the Gnostic Mass (Liber XV). Initiation proceeds through graded degrees, and ethics are individual: sin is defined solely as restriction of, or deviation from, one's True Will.
dossier.canon: The revealed 'Liber AL vel Legis' (1904) is central; the wider corpus comprises Crowley's 'Holy Books of Thelema,' 'Magick (Liber ABA / Book 4),' 'The Vision and the Voice' (Liber 418), the numbered libri of the A.'.A.'., and the Thoth Tarot.
dossier.reflects: The absolutizing of individual will — a religion that dissolves universal moral law into each person's unique inner trajectory and sanctifies self-determination itself.
sources: Hugh B. Urban, 'Magia Sexualis: Sex, Magic, and Liberation in Modern Western Esotericism' (2006)
Marco Pasi, 'Aleister Crowley and the Temptation of Politics' (2014)
Henrik Bogdan & Martin P. Starr (eds.), 'Aleister Crowley and Western Esotericism' (2012)
